content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 22, 1994

Daniel M. Kolkey
Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher
233 South Grand Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90071-3197

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-94-191

Dear Mr. Kolkey:

This is in response to your request for assistance regarding
the lobbying disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.¹
Since you have not identified your client, your letter is
considered a request for informal assistance pursuant to
Regulation 18329(c).²

QUESTION

Will your participation in making a proposal to the Export
Finance Board and any negotiations regarding the terms of that
proposal require your registration as a lobbyist?

CONCLUSION

Based upon the information you provided us, you have no
obligation to register as a lobbyist.

FACTS

Your client, a financial institution, seeks to assist in
establishing a new fund which would provide loan guarantees to
small and medium sized California companies to help support their
export activities. Government Code Sections 15390-15396.3

___________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sections
18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Kolkey, Daniel M.
File No. I-94-191
Page 2


authorized the Export Finance Program within the California World
Trade Commission. Under this program a fund was created which,
among other things, serves as collateral for guarantees for loans
to California small and medium sized companies involved in the
business of export. The amount of funds available for guarantees
is constrained by the balance available in the fund. Your client
is aware that the Export Finance Office, which administers the
program, would like to increase the amount available for
guarantees. Your client is interested in assisting in the
achievement of this goal by making, or arranging for the making,
of a contribution which would increase the funds available for
loan guarantees, subject to certain conditions. The conditions
would require that the additional funds be used to facilitate
California's exports to a particular country and your client would
also request a matching contribution from the current Export
Finance Fund.

The Export Finance Board supervises the California Export
Finance Office and the Export Finance Board is scheduled to meet
on June 30, 1994. I understand that it is anticipated that the
proposal will be made to the Export Finance Board at that time.

ANALYSIS

Government Code Section 82039 defines lobbyist as:

...any individual who is employed or contracts for
economic consideration, other than reimbursement for
reasonable travel expenses, to communicate directly or
through his or her agents with any elective state
official, agency official or legislative official for
the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative
action, if a substantial or regular portion of the
activities for which he or she received consideration is
for the purpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action...
(Emphasis added.)

Section 82037 defines "legislative action" as:

...the drafting, introduction, consideration,
modification, enactment or defeat of any bill,
resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other
matter by the Legislature or by either house of any
committee, subcommittee , joint or select committee
thereof, or by a member or employee of the Legislature
acting in his official capacity. "Legislative action"
also means the action of the Governor in approving or
vetoing any bill.

Section 82002 defines "administrative action" as:

... the proposal, drafting, development, consideration,
amendment, enactment or defeat by any state agency of

Kolkey, Daniel M.
File No. I-94-191
Page 3


any rule, regulation or other action in any rate-making
proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding, which
shall include any proceeding governed by Chapter 3.5,
(commencing with Section 11340) of Part I of Division 3
of Title 2.

The Export Finance Board's acceptance or non-acceptance of
your client's proposal is clearly not "legislative action" and
does not appear to fall within the definition of "administrative
action." By its definition, "administrative action" contemplates
that the agency is making a rule, adopting a regulation or taking
action which will be of general application to a group of persons.
Here, the Export Finance Board is being offered funds from your
client under certain conditions which do not establish new rules
with which businesses must comply. As such, the Board's action in
accepting funds is not a rule of general application and thus, not
"administrative action." Therefore, your activities before the
Board will not require you to register as a lobbyist under the
Act.

If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

Wayne P. Imberi
Political Reform Consultant

QUESTION: Will your participation in making a proposal to the Export
Finance Board and any negotiations regarding the terms of that
proposal require your registration as a lobbyist?

CONCLUSION: Based upon the information you provided us,
you have no
obligation to register as a lobbyist.
